column7: message

column7: Dunno.  Maybe it's too late, and I'll never turn off AdBlock.  Or maybe I'll get the general impression from news stories and Reddit posts in the future that ads have been toned down, and maybe then I'll turn it off then.  Regardless, the only way I can shape society in my image is by wielding any and all powers I possess without mercy, and right now AdBlock is one of those powers.  It's democracy, and it's evolution.

column7:
That would be bad, because people who may not want to for religious reasons and may be from another country aren't going to automatically assume their organs will be harvested without permission. I think the US would probably be one of the few places in the world where it would be done automatically, so there would be no reason for them to assume it was out of the ordinary. Organ donation is just that: a donation. You are not required to do it. Requiring it to do it basically says "as the government, by default I am entitled to do what I feel is best for your body after death unless you have already told me not to" and can definitely lead to people fighting about their rights. As it stands right now, we are of the understanding that the deceased person and then the family should have a say in what happens to a body before the government comes and tells them what to do with it. After all, the government does not foot the bill for most burials. 

It's messy, because people who are organ donors don't really care if their organs can't be used, but people who don't want to donate DO care if their organs do get used. So by default, you should be looking out for the ones who do care, because no matter the change, the ones that don't care won't be affected by your policy.

A lot of the issues around not being able to verify the status of someone (like no license available for example) could be solved with an opt-out policy instead of an opt-in policy. For example, everyone is assumed to be able to receive blood transfusions because it's an opt-out policy. Jehovah's Witnesses opt-out and carry special identifiers to show doctors that they opt-out.

A similar thing could be applied to organ donation. Everyone is assumed to be able to donate an organ unless you carry special identifier. Yes, this has its own slew of issues, but it would address the majority of those types of "I was never asked" cases.

Maybe we should just focus on living instead of worrying about what happens after we're gone. Just a thought!"

column7:
Some questions:

1.  How will you or the government know who has guns?  Legal owners who registered their gun may have records somewhere.  What about criminals who currently have illegal weapons?  How will the buy back program track criminals who owns illegal weapons?  

2.  Assuming all non-criminals who currently own guns participated,  this essentially takes all guns from law abiding citizens.  Will your gun buy back program include additional police officers who will be hired to round up all the illegal weapons out there?

1. 15 years is an excessive punishment for possession of a weapon, which is still a non-violent crime (I'm pretty sure sex offenders typically don't get that much). Our prisons are already overcrowded with non-violent offenders. Your proposal is taken directly from the War on Drugs playbook. 

2. Ex post facto laws are prohibited by the Constitution (Article 1, Section 9, Clause 3). 

You've convinced me, OP. Convinced me that you're an authoritarian bootlicker looking to oppress the innocent.

"@freezing-flight Oh, wow, calling me an authoritarian bootlicker? That's a new one. I guess you missed the part where I was asking for a practical solution to gun control, not advocating for a dystopian future. But sure, let's just throw around labels instead of having a real discussion. Maybe we should just stick to talking about the weather, it's safer. And @hoary-soccer, I'm sure criminals will just line up to turn in their guns, right? Because they're known for their impeccable law-abiding behavior. Let's all just keep dreaming."
@fried-crest Let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on the topic. Personal attacks are not constructive. Let's discuss gun control solutions without resorting to name-calling.
